__comment: EuroTransit order trace dashboard — paste an order ID, find the trace(s) whose spans carry span.order.id (checkout request, every Kafka consumer stage, Payments authorize), select a result, and render the complete trace traversal in an embedded Grafana traces panel. One row = healthy single trace; multiple rows = the chain broke at a stage boundary.
title: EuroTransit — Trace an order

list.description: Paste the orderId returned by POST /orders.
list.label: Order ID
list.name: order_id

list.description: Set by the search-result link; drives the trace waterfall.
list.label: Trace ID
list.name: trace_id

panels.title: Follow one order through the money path

options.content:
### 1. Paste the order ID above · 2. Select the matching row · 3. Read the waterfall below
The expected happy path is **Orders accept → `order-placed` → Inventory reserve → `inventory-reserved` → Orders call Payments (`POST /payments/authorize`, sync + traceparent) → `payment-authorized` → Orders confirm → `order-confirmed` → Notifications** — normally ONE trace rooted at the checkout `POST /orders` span.

Every stage tags `span.order.id` (checkout request, each Kafka consumer, the Payments authorize span), so if the search returns **multiple rows for one order**, the trace chain is broken at the stage where one fragment ends and the next begins — that is a finding, not a search problem. The stage table below lists every tagged span across all fragments.

`order.id` is a high-cardinality Tempo attribute; it is deliberately **not** a Prometheus label. Widen the time range if the order is older than the 6-hour view (Tempo retains 48 h). A trace keeps receiving Kafka spans after the HTTP root span ends, so refresh while an order is still progressing.

panels.title: Traces matching order $order_id
panels.description: Traces containing at least one span tagged with order.id — normally exactly one, rooted at the Orders checkout request. Multiple rows mean the producer/consumer trace chain broke between stages. Select any cell and choose 'Show trace path below' to populate the embedded waterfall.

panels.title: Order $order_id — tagged stage spans
panels.description: Every span carrying span.order.id, across ALL trace fragments: checkout request, each Kafka consumer stage, and the Payments authorize call. Read it top-to-bottom as the order's journey; a stage missing here means that stage has not run (or failed before its span was tagged).

panels.title: Order $order_id — complete trace path
panels.description: Full trace traversal for the selected trace ID. Expand spans to inspect service, Kafka, HTTP, duration, status, and attributes; use the critical-path control to highlight the latency-dominant chain.
